sectionSubtitle: Real products solving real problems – from conception to production

items.title: Supatrack
items.description: The nutrition app that actually understands what you're eating – snap a photo, get insights
items.longDescription: Started as a 'what if we could make calorie counting not suck?' conversation. Now it's a full-scale mobile app with ML-powered food recognition that actually works. Leading a team of 4 developers, we're building something people genuinely want to use daily – not just download and forget.
highlights: Architected the entire system from scratch – picked the tech stack, designed the database schema, and set up CI/CD pipelines that actually prevent 3 AM disasters
Built a FastAPI backend that handles 10,000+ daily requests without breaking a sweat (load tested with Locust to be sure)
Implemented smart caching with Redis that cut our API response times by 70% – because nobody likes waiting for their food to log
Integrated TensorFlow Lite models directly into Flutter for offline food recognition – works even in your gym's terrible WiFi
Deployed to both Google Play Store and Apple App Store, dealing with their review processes (iOS review taught me patience)
Set up real production monitoring with Sentry and CloudWatch – we know about bugs before users report them
Mentored 2 junior developers who now handle features independently (proud teacher moment)

items.title: Open Food Facts Contributions
items.description: Making the world's food database better, one pull request at a time
items.longDescription: Jumped into Open Food Facts because I believe nutrition data should be free and accessible. Started by fixing a tiny UI bug, now I'm neck-deep in improving how the platform recognizes and categorizes food products. It's Wikipedia for food, and I'm helping make it better.
highlights: Fixed that annoying search bar that wouldn't stay sticky on mobile – small fix, big UX win
Improved food barcode scanning accuracy by 15% through better error handling and user feedback
Added dark mode support because developers have eyes too (most popular PR to date)
Collaborated with contributors from 12 different countries – code reviews in broken English are surprisingly effective
